FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from 3 waves (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen use (total daily screen time calculated as weighted mean across weekdays/weekends from Youth Screen Time Survey)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models testing associations between parental factors and sleep outcomes, with screen use as mediator; mediation analyses with path coefficients reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported: parental monitoring β=0.04 (95% CI 0.01-0.07; P=.04), family conflict β=-0.04 (95% CI -0.07 to -0.01; P=.04), parental psychopathology β=-0.29 (95% CI -0.31 to -0.26; P<.001), plus mediation path coefficients

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort study with preregistered design analyzing parental factors at wave 1 predicting sleep outcomes at wave 3 (4 years later)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in JAMA Network Open, September 11, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes multiple non-SM outcomes (sleep is primary outcome: duration, timing, chronotype, quality, regularity). Parental factors → sleep is the main association; screen use and emotional regulation are mediators, not outcomes. Multiple non-SM outcomes present.
notes: Parenting/family factors (warmth, monitoring, conflict, psychopathology) are predictors. Cross-sectional mediation component within longitudinal design. Sex moderation also tested.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 5.1 data from baseline and year 4 waves; N=4,754 youth with available data

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time (average daily hours) and problematic screen use (PSU) for three screen activities: video games, social media, and smartphones

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models testing whether baseline impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, and cognitive ability predict screen time and PSU; multiple test statistics reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Results section reports associations with p-values and coefficient directions (e.g., 'ST in mid-adolescence was positively predicted by baseline urgency... and negatively predicted by cognitive ability'); specific numeric values in methods describe scales (e.g., PSU cutoffs)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis with linear mixed-effects models examining predictive associations from late childhood to mid-adolescence

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in JAACAP Open, 2025, Volume 3, pages 1259-1269

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/clinical trait predictors (impulsivity facets [urgency, sensation-seeking, lack of planning, lack of perseverance], reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity, externalizing, internalizing, cognitive ability). E16 applies: individual-characteristic predictors of phone/SM use MET regardless of direction/timeframe. Not a demographic-only FT-EC7 case.
notes: Prospective design with individual differences measured at ages 9-10 predicting screen use at ages 12-15. Distinguishes between screen time hours and problematic use patterns.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD dataset baseline data; N=9,337 individuals with social media use and diffusion-weighted MRI data

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures daily social media consumption categorized as <30 minutes/day (n=8,868) vs >30 minutes/day (n=455)

FT_IC3.evidence: Odds ratios assess influence of demographic/neighborhood characteristics on social media use; linear regressions examine effects of social media consumption on white matter fractional anisotropy (FA)

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios reported for demographic/socioeconomic predictors of social media use (e.g., female sex, black race, puberty, lower household income, insufficient sleep all significantly associated with p<0.05, OR>1.5)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis with 9,337 baseline participants; linear regressions and odds ratio analyses on white matter structure

FT_IC6.evidence: Published abstract in Biological Psychiatry, May 1, 2025, plenary session

FT_EC7_reason: Paper examines social media consumption → white matter health (neurobiological outcome), not just demographic predictors of SM use. White matter structure/integrity is a neural/biological outcome, making this a smartphone/SM → non-SM-outcome analysis. Cross-sectional design also precludes FT-EC7.
notes: Cross-sectional baseline analysis. White matter analyzed across 34 tracts. Key finding: associations between SM consumption and FA became non-significant after adjusting for race and SES, suggesting demographic/socioeconomic factors more critical than SM use itself for white matter health.
